questions.question: What is the primary management-architecture difference between a traditional on-premises managed network and a cloud-managed network (e.g., Cisco Meraki)?
choices: On-premises devices cannot be managed remotely at all
Cloud-managed devices send configuration and telemetry to a vendor-hosted dashboard accessed over the internet, instead of a locally hosted controller
Cloud-managed networks do not support VLANs
On-premises networks cannot use SNMP
questions.explanation: Cloud-based management moves the management plane (dashboard, configuration database, analytics) to a vendor-hosted cloud service reached over the internet, versus a locally hosted controller or per-device CLI/SNMP management on-prem.

correct.explanation: Cloud-based management moves the management plane (dashboard, configuration database, analytics) to a vendor-hosted cloud service reached over the internet, versus a locally hosted controller or per-device CLI/SNMP management on-prem.

incorrect.explanation: **On-premises devices cannot be managed remotely at all** contradicts the expected behavior. Cloud-based management moves the management plane (dashboard, configuration database, analytics) to a vendor-hosted cloud service reached over the internet, versus a locally hosted controller or per-device CLI/SNMP management on-prem.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: **Cloud-managed networks do not support VLANs** contradicts the expected behavior. Cloud-based management moves the management plane (dashboard, configuration database, analytics) to a vendor-hosted cloud service reached over the internet, versus a locally hosted controller or per-device CLI/SNMP management on-prem.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: **On-premises networks cannot use SNMP** contradicts the expected behavior. questions.question: A company with 50 branch offices wants a single dashboard to monitor and configure all branch switches and APs without establishing a VPN to each site individually. Which management approach best fits this requirement?
choices: Local CLI management via console cable at each site
A cloud-based management platform (e.g., Meraki dashboard)
SNMPv1 polling from a central NMS
Telnet to each device's management IP
questions.explanation: Cloud-based management platforms are designed for exactly this scenario: centralized, single-pane-of-glass visibility and configuration across many geographically distributed sites without per-site VPN tunnels.

questions.question: An organization's cloud-managed access points lose connectivity to the cloud dashboard during an ISP outage at headquarters. What is the most accurate description of the impact on the branch network?
choices: All wireless clients are immediately disconnected
The APs typically continue forwarding existing traffic (data plane unaffected), but configuration changes and cloud analytics are unavailable until connectivity is restored
The APs factory-reset themselves automatically
Wired switches in the same branch also stop forwarding traffic
questions.explanation: Most cloud-managed architectures separate the data plane (local forwarding continues independently) from the management/control plane (cloud dashboard). A WAN/internet outage interrupts management visibility and configuration push, but local forwarding generally continues using the last-known configuration.
questions.concept: cloud management data vs control plane

incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: **The APs factory-reset themselves automatically** describes a different mechanism than this question tests. questions.question: Which of the following is a typical advantage of cloud-based device management over traditional local management?
choices: Lower latency for all management operations regardless of internet connectivity
Zero-touch provisioning — new devices can be shipped to a site and automatically pull their configuration from the cloud on first boot
No internet connection is ever required
Configuration changes never require any internet access
questions.explanation: A hallmark benefit of cloud management is zero-touch provisioning: a device shipped to a remote site can connect to the internet, phone home to the cloud controller, and automatically receive its configuration — no on-site CLI work needed.

questions.question: Which statement correctly contrasts Cisco DNA Center with Cisco Meraki dashboard in terms of deployment model?
choices: DNA Center is cloud-only; Meraki is on-premises-only
DNA Center is typically deployed on-premises (or as a private cloud) as a local controller, while Meraki is a vendor-hosted cloud dashboard
Both are exclusively command-line tools with no GUI
Both require the same physical hardware appliance

questions.question: Which of the following is a typical characteristic of traditional local (on-premises) network management?
choices: Devices are managed individually via CLI, SNMP, or a locally-hosted controller, often requiring per-site or per-device access
All configuration is pushed automatically from a vendor's global cloud
Firmware updates are applied automatically across all sites with no administrator involvement
Management is impossible without a dedicated WAN link to each branch

questions.question: An engineer says, "Our network uses a single pane of glass for management." What does this phrase mean?
choices: All devices share one physical management cable
A single user interface or dashboard provides visibility and control over the entire network's devices and sites
Only one administrator account exists for the whole network

questions.question: Which scenario is the BEST fit for traditional local management rather than cloud-based management?
choices: A multinational retail chain with 500 small stores needing rapid, uniform rollout of new APs
A single highly-regulated data center where all management traffic must remain on an isolated, air-gapped internal network with no internet access
A startup that wants to add new branch offices with minimal on-site IT staff
A school district wanting centralized visibility across 30 campuses
questions.explanation: Cloud management depends on internet connectivity to a vendor's dashboard. An air-gapped, highly regulated environment with no internet access is the classic case where local/on-premises management is required instead.
